retrieved_docs.title: COX Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Hypersensitivity Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: NSAIDs are among the most common medications to cause hypersensitivity reactions. The number of hypersensitivity reactions caused by NSAIDs is extensive and complex, and classification is evolving. Hypersensitivity reactions vary widely in presentation and mechanism. They can be due to a specific drug, termed selective responder in the literature, or due to any NSAID, termed cross-intolerant. Clinical manifestations include urticaria, rhinitis, asthma, angioedema, bullous or desquamating skin lesions, DRESS syndrome, or toxic epidermal necrolysis. Some reactions are organ-specific clinical entities, causing bile duct syndrome, meningitis, or vasculitis. Mechanisms can be IgE-mediated, T cell-mediated, prostaglandin inhibition-mediated, or are often unknown. Other possible causes are present as well, and clinicians may miss NSAIDs as the primary cause. Ibuprofen is the most common among all NSAIDs to cause a hypersensitivity reaction.

retrieved_docs.title: Pain Management Medications -- Adverse Effects -- Nonopioid analgesic agents
retrieved_docs.content: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) : This drug is a safe and effective medication when used correctly. Documented adverse effects depend on the route of administration. If administered orally or rectally, acetaminophen may cause any of the following: Rash or hypersensitivity reactions (toxic epidermal necrolysis, acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis, and Stevens-Johnson syndrome) Hematological: anemia, leukopenia, neutropenia, pancytopenia Nephrotoxicity Metabolic and electrolyte disorders Decreased serum bicarbonate Hyponatremia Hypocalcemia Hyperammonemia Hyperchloremia Hyperuricemia Hyperglycemia Hyperbilirubinemia Elevated alkaline phosphatase [5] [6] [27]

retrieved_docs.content: Drugs are most frequently implicated in AGEP, as immune-mediated reactions to drugs are more consistent and reproducible than responses to other reported triggers. Early recognition and withdrawal of the causative drug remain the most effective strategies to hasten resolution and prevent complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1676
retrieved_docs.content: Mandelic acid adverse reaction refers to a harmful or unwanted response that occurs as a direct result of exposure to mandelic acid, indicating that mandelic acid itself is the causative agent of this reaction. Recognizing mandelic acid as the trigger is essential for clinicians when evaluating patients who experience skin irritation, allergic responses, or other negative effects after using products containing this alpha‑hydroxy acid. Proper identification of mandelic acid as the underlying cause guides appropriate management, such as discontinuing the offending agent and providing symptomatic treatment.

Recognizing hypersensitivity as a contraindicating factor for these medications is essential for safe prescribing and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa, centrally acting adntiadrenergic agents is classified as an antiadrenergic agents, centrally acting (using an “isa” relationship) and is a member of the broader methyldopa category. Both methyldopa and the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug fenoprofen have a contraindication for patients with فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), indicating that these agents should not be used in individuals who are hypersensitive. Similarly, the antifungal medication butenafine is contraindicated with the disease فرط تحسس, reinforcing the need to assess hypersensitivity status before prescribing these drugs. Recognizing and avoiding hypersensitivity reactions to methyldopa, fenoprofen, and butenafine is essential for safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1147
retrieved_docs.content: its topical uses, ketoconazole has important drug‑interaction considerations. The medication classes dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are contraindicated when ketoconazole is present, reflecting a risk of adverse pharmacologic interaction. Likewise, the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist ubrogepant is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, underscoring the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication list before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products. Recognizing both the extensive range of ketoconazole‑based dermatologic therapies and its contraindications with specific systemic agents is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin, a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug, is often co‑administered with ranitidine, an H2‑receptor antagonist, to reduce the risk of gastric irritation that can accompany aspirin therapy. Nevertheless, aspirin is contraindicated in preschool‑aged children because of the heightened risk of serious adverse events such as Reye syndrome. Therefore, while the combination of aspirin and ranitidine may be appropriate for many adult patients, clinicians must avoid prescribing aspirin to preschool children and consider age‑specific contraindications when planning treatment regimens.
